steps.title: Define variables and constraints
steps.content:
Let $s$ = number of small jars and $l$ = number of large jars. The problem gives us two key constraints:
  • Cost constraint: $5.20s + 12.40l \leq 2500$
  • Quantity constraint: $s + l \geq 220$ (minimum for discounted pricing)
We want to maximize $l$.

steps.title: Minimize small jars to maximize large jars
steps.content:
To get the most large jars for the budget, we need the fewest small jars.

The quantity constraint $s + l \geq 220$ creates a minimum threshold.

To maximize $l$, we operate at the boundary where $s + l = 220$:
$$s = 220 - l$$
This substitution will express the cost constraint in terms of $l$ alone.

steps.title: Solve for the maximum number of large jars
steps.content:
Substitute $s = 220 - l$ into the cost constraint:
$$5.20(220 - l) + 12.40l \leq 2500$$
Expanding:
$$1144 - 5.20l + 12.40l \leq 2500$$
Combining like terms:
$$1144 + 7.20l \leq 2500$$
Subtracting 1144 from both sides:
$$7.20l \leq 1356$$
Dividing by 7.20:
$$l \leq \frac{1356}{7.20} \approx 188.33$$
Since the manager can only purchase whole jars, the maximum number of large jars is 188.
